content.full_text:
86100

96.369

Richard C. Bartlett                    (405) 263-5000
Alabama Wood Employees Assn.

Phone Conversation on July 27, 1976

We discussed the regulations requiring receipt of
vouchers. Displayed the type of voucher he may
prepare. He suggested we require no receipt
for general maintenance effort. I told him we
would consider this where we review the regulations


Ruth Eshman
7/27/76

[The image shows a blank lined yellow notepad/paper with two white holes punched at the top, presumably for hanging or fastening. There is no visible text to transcribe on this page.]

csea                    CALIFORNIA SCHOOL EMPLOYEES ASSOCIATION
     2350 PARAGON DRIVE      P.O. BOX 640      SAN JOSE, CA 95106      TELEPHONE (408) 263-8000

                                                                July 16, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Honorable Members:

Auditing of my records was recently completed by Mr. Michael Rand, Political
Reform Audit Program, Franchise Tax Board.

I was informed that my records and record-keeping were adequate in all respects
except for:

1. Failure to report two items in a timely manner (January, 1975), but which
   were corrected by April, 1975.

2. Failure to maintain receipts for meals purchased not involving expenditures
   for others. In other words, receipts for meals purchased for myself during
   times that I was functioning as a legislative advocate.

Since the Commission rules require the maintenance of such receipts, I presume
I have, technically, failed to comply.

The purpose of the law and rules, as I understand them, is to provide adequate
disclosure of expenditures made by an advocate in his/her efforts to influence
legislation. The intent, I trust, is not the retention of useless receipts which
fail to "disclose" anything, but is unnecessarily burdensome to the advocate, the
auditor, and anybody else involved in true "disclosure".

I am attaching a copy of those expenditures (for meals) that I have made over
the audited period. All are for individual meals and, as you will note, I
tend not to be a "big" spender. I should note that bridge toll receipts are
also missing.

I suggest that this type of problem can be resolved to the satisfaction of all
concerned while complying with the intent and spirit of the law. My suggestions
are as follows:

1. Receipts for bridge tolls should not be required. They have to be paid and
   everybody knows exactly what the fees are. What possible purpose is served
   by requiring a receipt for same?

2. The Commission should establish a reasonable maximum cost per meal, whereby
   the lobbyist is still required to report where, when, and how much, but is
   not required to maintain a receipt provided, of course, the meal is purchased
   by and for the lobbyist. For example, the Commission might establish a
   breakfast schedule of $4; lunch $4.50; dinner $7.50, or some other appropriate
   schedule.

[The image appears to be a very faint or low-contrast scanned document. The text is extremely difficult to read clearly. Most of the content is illegible or too faint to transcribe with confidence. There appear to be several paragraphs of text, but individual words and sentences cannot be reliably distinguished.]

Fair Political Practices Commission
July 16, 1976
Page two


If the retention of such receipts serves any useful purpose, I fail to understand
what it could possibly be. It appears to me to be an exercise in record-keeping
for the sole purpose of record-keeping.

May I respectfully urge the Commission to study these suggestions and develop a
reasonable solution. Certainly, I want to comply with the intent and letter of
the law, but I don't believe a lobbyist, the auditor, or Commission should be
burdened with trivia that serves no purpose except as an irritant --- there are
enough necessary irritants without the senseless variety.

Respectfully,

CALIFORNIA SCHOOL EMPLOYEES ASSOCIATION

[signature]
Richard C. Bartlett
Executive Director

RCB/avt

cc: Mr. Michael Rand

Enclosure

embedding.qa_text: Richard C. Bartlett California School Employees Assn. Phone conversation on July 27, 1976 We discussed the regulations requiring receipts and vouchers. I explained the type of vouchers he may prepare. He suggested we require no receipts for personal maintenance items. I told him we would consider this when we review the regulations. Rich Eichman 7/27/76 This image shows a sheet of yellow lined paper with blue lines, commonly used for writing or note-taking. The paper has two holes at the top, likely for binding into a notebook or folder. There is no text or content on this page. **CALIFORNIA SCHOOL EMPLOYEES ASSOCIATION** 2350 PARAGON DRIVE P.O. BOX 640 SAN JOSE, CA 95106 TELEPHONE (408) 263-8000 July 16, 1976 Fair Political Practices Commission Post Office Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Honorable Members: Auditing of my records was recently completed by Mr. Michael Rand, Political Reform Audit Program, Franchise Tax Board. I was informed that my records and record-keeping were adequate in all respects except for: 1. Failure to report two items in a timely manner (January, 1975), but which were corrected by April, 1975. 2. Failure to maintain receipts for meals purchased not involving expenditures for others. In other words, receipts for meals purchased for myself during times that I was functioning as a legislative advocate. Since the Commission rules require the maintenance of such receipts, I presume I have, technically, failed to comply. The purpose of the law and rules, as I understand them, is to provide adequate disclosure of expenditures made by an advocate in his/her efforts to influence legislation. The intent, I trust, is not the retention of useless receipts which fail to "disclose" anything, but is unnecessarily burdensome to the advocate, the auditor, and anybody else involved in true "disclosure". I am attaching a copy of those expenditures (for meals) that I have made over the audited period. All are for individual meals and, as you will note, I tend not to be a "big" spender. I should note that bridge toll receipts are also missing. I suggest that this type of problem can be resolved to the satisfaction of all concerned while complying with the intent and spirit of the law. My suggestions are as follows: 1. Receipts for bridge tolls should not be required. They have to be paid and everybody knows exactly what the fees are. What possible purpose is served by requiring a receipt for same? 2. The Commission should establish a reasonable maximum cost per meal, whereby the lobbyist is still required to report where, when, and how much, but is not required to maintain a receipt provided, of course, the meal is purchased by and for the lobbyist. For example, the Commission might establish a breakfast schedule of $4; lunch $4.50; dinner $7.50, or some other appropriate schedule.
